description: The image displays a CrossMark 'Check for updates' user-interface badge featuring a circular emblem with a red bookmark-shaped icon set against grey, blue, and yellow circular segments, accompanied by the text 'Check for updates' beneath it. This element is a publisher interface icon rather than a scientific figure; it contains no materials science data such as SEM micrographs, XRD patterns, electrochemical polarization curves, EIS Nyquist plots, or SKPFM potential maps. No sample identifiers (e.g., S0, S950, S1050, S1150), solution treatment temperatures, grain size measurements, oxide film thickness values, or corrosion rate numbers are visually present. Consequently, the image cannot be used to extract trends linking grain size optimization to Al-rich oxide film formation and enhanced corrosion resistance in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The visual content is strictly limited to the update-check iconography, and no scientific conclusions can be drawn from this image alone.

This figure presents stacked X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) under four processing states: as-hot-rolled S0 without solution treatment (red), and solution-treated samples S950 at 950 °C (green), S1050 at 1050 °C (blue), and S1150 at 1150 °C (pink), each held for 2 h and water-quenched. All four patterns exhibit single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) austenite, as confirmed by diffraction peaks at (111), (200), (220), and (311) marked with orange diamonds. The as-rolled S0 shows distinct relative intensities, while solution treatment systematically enhances the (111) peak intensity and reduces the (220) peak intensity with increasing temperature. Accompanying this texture evolution, grain size increases from approximately 8.7 μm in S950 to 32.0 μm in S1150. These results demonstrate that solution treatment preserves the single FCC austenitic phase while modifying relative peak intensities, establishing a phase-pure matrix for investigating grain-size effects on Al-rich oxide film formation and corrosion resistance.

Figure 2 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) under different solution treatment conditions. The caption identifies panel (a) as S0 in the as-hot-rolled state without solution treatment, displaying a nonuniform, deformed microstructure with fragmented grains. Panels (b), (c), and (d) correspond to samples S950, S1050, and S1150 after solution treatment at 950 °C, 1050 °C, and 1150 °C for 2 h with water quenching, respectively. Visually, the images reveal a progressive transition from fine equiaxed austenitic grains with abundant annealing twin boundaries in S950 to intermediate-sized grains in S1050 and coarse equiaxed grains in S1150, accompanied by a marked reduction in twin boundary density and grain boundary area as grain coarsening occurs. Published measurements report average grain sizes of approximately 8.7 μm for S950 and 32.0 μm for S1150. All solution-treated samples exhibit fully recrystallized microstructures, contrasting with the retained deformation structure in S0. This systematic grain size and grain boundary density variation establishes the microstructural foundation supporting the core conclusion that fine-grained structures promote dense, Al-rich oxide film formation and thereby enhance corrosion resistance.

Figure 3 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs), comprising inverse pole figure (IPF), kernel average misorientation (KAM), and grain boundary (GB) distribution maps for the as-hot-rolled S0 sample and samples solution-treated at 950 °C (S950), 1050 °C (S1050), and 1150 °C (S1150). The IPF maps (a–d) reveal that S0 possesses a nonuniform grain structure with small fragmented grains at large grain interfaces, whereas solution treatment homogenizes the microstructure into equiaxed austenite grains; caption reports the average grain size progressively increases with temperature, reaching 32.0 μm in S1150. The KAM maps (a₁–d₁) show that S0 exhibits pronounced residual stress (caption reports average KAM 1.62), while dynamic recrystallization during solution treatment reduces the KAM values to approximately 0.4 for all treated samples. The GB distribution maps (a₂–d₂) indicate that S0 contains abundant low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 2–10°), whereas recrystallized S950, S1050, and S1150 display abundant annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, Σ3) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, >10°), with caption reporting the maximum ATB and HAGB densities in S950 (0.146 and 0.114 μm/μm², respectively) that progressively decrease with increasing solution temperature. These microstructural observations demonstrate that solution treatment modulates grain size, residual stress, and GB character, establishing the microstructural basis for corrosion resistance optimization.

Figure 4 presents grain size distributions and grain boundary (GB) statistics for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) across four processing states. Subfigures (a)-(d) display relative proportion histograms versus grain size for S0, S950, S1050, and S1150, reporting average grain sizes of 7.6, 8.7, 21.1, and 32.0 μm, respectively. The distributions show that solution treatment homogenizes the microstructure while higher temperatures progressively coarsen the grains. Subfigure (e) compares annealing twin boundary (ATB) and high-angle grain boundary (HAGB) densities together with average kernel average misorientation (KAM) values. S950 exhibits the peak ATB and HAGB densities at 0.146 and 0.114 μm/μm², which monotonically decrease to 0.040 and 0.019 μm/μm² in S1150. The average KAM value falls sharply from approximately 1.62 in the as-rolled S0 to about 0.4 after solution treatment, indicating substantial residual stress relief via recrystallization. Collectively, the data establish that moderate solution treatment refines and homogenizes the grain structure with high GB density and low residual stress, whereas excessive temperature causes grain coarsening and boundary density reduction.

This figure presents TEM characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs). Panel (a) is a bright-field TEM image of the as-hot-rolled S0 sample showing numerous κ-carbides (5–10 nm in diameter, indicated by yellow arrows) irregularly distributed within the austenitic matrix. Panel (a1) provides the corresponding HRTEM lattice image and FFT pattern indexed along the [110] zone axis, confirming the crystalline nature of the κ-carbide precipitates. Panel (a2) shows the high-density, irregularly distributed dislocations in S0 and an SAED pattern of the austenite matrix along [110]. Panel (b) displays the twin morphology after solution treatment, with SAED patterns revealing both matrix and twin reflections along [110], indicating abundant annealing twin boundaries (ATBs). The figure demonstrates that the as-rolled microstructure contains nanoscale κ-carbides and high dislocation density, whereas solution treatment reduces dislocation density and introduces ATBs that effectively hinder dislocation movement, supporting the grain size optimization strategy for enhancing corrosion resistance through Al-rich oxide film formation.

Figure 6 presents the electrochemical corrosion characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panel (a) displays potentiodynamic polarization curves for four samples: S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950 °C), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050 °C), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150 °C). The curves reveal active dissolution regions, followed by current density decreases attributed to surface oxide film formation, and subsequent passive-like behaviors at higher potentials. Panel (b) quantifies the corresponding breakdown current densities (i_bre), showing S950 achieves the lowest value of 2.72 μA/cm², significantly lower than S1050 (6.55 μA/cm²), S1150 (8.84 μA/cm²), and S0 (9.92 μA/cm²). This trend demonstrates that fine-grained microstructures obtained by low-temperature solution treatment markedly enhance corrosion resistance compared to both the as-rolled and coarse-grained conditions, supporting the conclusion that optimized grain size promotes the formation of a protective Al-rich oxide film.

This figure comprises four SEM micrographs showing the surface corrosion morphology of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiodynamic polarization to 5 mA/cm² in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panel (a) presents sample S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), exhibiting extensive corrosion damage with numerous deep pits attributed to high residual stress and heterogeneous grain boundary distribution. Panel (b) shows sample S950 (solution treated at 950 °C for 2 h), where only slight pitting corrosion with smaller pit sizes is visible, indicating superior corrosion resistance. Panel (c) corresponds to sample S1050 (solution treated at 1050 °C), displaying an increased number and size of corrosion pits compared with S950, with an inset providing a magnified view of an individual pit at 10 μm scale. Panel (d) depicts sample S1150 (solution treated at 1150 °C), revealing significant pitting corrosion expansion on pit walls and larger pits. The overall comparison demonstrates that increasing solution treatment temperature and grain coarsening from S950 to S1150 monotonically degrades pitting resistance, whereas the fine-grained S950 microstructure facilitates the formation of a dense Al-rich oxide film, yielding the highest corrosion resistance among all samples.

This figure presents the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comprising three panels: (a) Nyquist plot, (b) Bode magnitude plot, and (c) Bode phase plot. The data compare four samples—S0 (as-hot-rolled), S950 (solution-treated at 950 °C), S1050 (1050 °C), and S1150 (1150 °C)—with solid fitting lines derived from an equivalent circuit model Rs-(R1Q1)-(R2Q2) shown in the inset of (a), where constant phase elements Q account for nonideal capacitive behavior. In the Nyquist plot, S950 exhibits the largest depressed semicircle, indicating the highest polarization resistance, while S0 and S1150 show smaller arcs. The Bode plots confirm two time constants with a maximum phase angle between 65° and 75° at intermediate frequencies, reflecting surface porosity and film inhomogeneity. Overall, the EIS data demonstrate that fine-grained S950 possesses superior corrosion resistance due to enhanced formation of a dense Al-rich oxide film, whereas coarser grains or the as-rolled condition degrade the protective properties.

Figure 9 presents a bar chart comparing the corrosion rates of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after 30 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Four samples are evaluated: as-hot-rolled S0 without solution treatment, and S950, S1050, and S1150 solution-treated at 950°C, 1050°C, and 1150°C for 2 h, respectively. The measured corrosion rates are 0.326 mm/y for S0, 0.160 mm/y for S950, 0.248 mm/y for S1050, and 0.281 mm/y for S1150, with error bars indicating experimental variability. The results reveal a non-monotonic dependence on solution treatment temperature: the fine-grained S950 exhibits the lowest corrosion rate, approximately 51% lower than S0 and 43% lower than the coarse-grained S1150. As the solution treatment temperature increases to 1050°C and 1150°C, the corrosion rate progressively rises toward the as-rolled level. This trend supports the core conclusion that grain size optimization—specifically the fine-grained microstructure with high grain boundary density in S950—promotes rapid formation of a dense, Al-rich oxide film, thereby significantly enhancing corrosion resistance compared to both the as-rolled and coarse-grained conditions.

This figure displays potentiostatic polarization curves showing current density versus time for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comparing four samples: S0 (as-hot-rolled, red), S950 (solution-treated at 950 °C, green), S1050 (1050 °C, blue), and S1150 (1150 °C, magenta). The main plot spans 0–8000 s with current density in units of ×10⁻⁴ A/cm², and an inset magnifies the steady-state region from 6400–7400 s. All curves exhibit a rapid current drop within the first 60 s to below 9 μA/cm², corresponding to initial oxide film formation, followed by transient increases and decreases during 600–1400 s that reflect oxide film rupture and self-healing. The stabilized current densities rank as S0 (8.39 μA/cm²) > S1150 (6.04 μA/cm²) > S1050 (4.52 μA/cm²) > S950 (2.45 μA/cm²), indicating that the finest-grained S950 sample achieves the lowest stable current density and thus the most protective oxide film, while higher solution-treatment temperatures producing larger grains progressively reduce corrosion resistance.

Figure 11 presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panel (a) is a Nyquist plot showing capacitive arcs for the as-hot-rolled sample S0 and solution-treated samples S950 (950 °C), S1050 (1050 °C), and S1150 (1150 °C), together with the Rs-(R1Q1)-(R2Q2) equivalent circuit inset used for fitting. Panel (b) is the Bode magnitude plot of impedance modulus versus frequency, and panel (c) is the Bode phase plot versus frequency. The S950 sample displays the largest capacitive arc radius, the highest low-frequency impedance modulus, and the highest phase angle sustained over the widest frequency range, indicating the most stable, uniform, and protective oxide film. Conversely, S0 shows the smallest arc and lowest impedance. With increasing solution-treatment temperature and grain growth from S950 to S1150, the EIS characteristics progressively weaken, confirming that fine-grained microstructures favor the formation of a denser Al-rich oxide film with superior corrosion resistance.

Figure 12 presents Mott-Schottky analysis and carrier density data for low-density steels (LDSs) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panel (a) plots C^-2 versus applied potential (E vs. SCE) for the as-hot-rolled sample S0 and solution-treated samples S950, S1050, and S1150, with a dashed line separating Region 1 and Region 2. Caption reports that Region 1 (-1.13 to -0.93 V_SCE) displays positive slopes characteristic of n-type semiconductors, whereas Region 2 (-0.93 to -0.67 V_SCE) exhibits negative slopes indicating p-type behavior. The S950 curve reaches the highest C^-2 peak in the n-type region, while S0 shows the lowest. Panel (b) reports the calculated donor density (N_D) and acceptor density (N_A). S0 registers the highest defect concentrations (N_D = 0.70 x 10^21 cm^-3, N_A = 2.25 x 10^21 cm^-3), whereas S950 records the lowest (N_D = 0.26 x 10^21 cm^-3, N_A = 0.68 x 10^21 cm^-3). As the solution-treatment temperature rises from 950 to 1150 °C, both N_D and N_A increase progressively yet remain below the S0 level. These findings indicate that the finest grain structure (S950) minimizes oxide-film defect densities, thereby reducing penetration channels for chloride ions and improving corrosion resistance relative to coarser-grained conditions.

This figure presents XPS spectra of Fe 2p3/2, Mn 2p3/2, Al 2p, and O 1s core levels for the oxide films formed on low-density steels (LDSs) after 7200 s potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The four columns correspond to samples S0 (as-hot-rolled), S950, S1050, and S1150 (solution-treated at 950–1150 °C), while rows (a–d), (a1–d1), (a2–d2), and (a3–d3) show the deconvoluted spectra for Fe, Mn, Al, and O, respectively. The Fe 2p3/2 spectra reveal Fe²⁺ (709.8 eV), Fe³⁺_ox (711.5 eV), and Fe³⁺_hy (713.3 eV) in all samples, with S950 uniquely exhibiting an Fe²⁺ satellite peak. Mn 2p3/2 shows Mn²⁺, Mn⁴⁺, and a satellite peak in S0, S1050, and S1150, whereas S950 displays only Mn²⁺ (640.4 eV). Al 2p spectra exhibit a single Al³⁺ peak (74.1 eV) across all samples, and O 1s spectra are resolved into O²⁻ (529.7 eV), OH⁻ (531.6 eV), and H₂O (533.2 eV) components. The data demonstrate that fine-grained S950 forms an Al-rich oxide film with the highest Al³⁺ signal and suppressed Mn⁴⁺, supporting its superior corrosion resistance compared to coarse-grained counterparts.

Figure 14 displays a 3D stacked bar chart of the relative cation fraction (at.%) of different metal valence states in the oxide films of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs), derived from XPS peak-area analysis. The x-axis shows aggregated elemental reference columns for Al, Fe, and Mn indicating overall valence contributions, followed by individual sample columns: S0 (as-hot-rolled), S950, S1050, and S1150 (solution-treated at 950–1150 °C). The legend identifies Al³⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺_ox, Fe³⁺_hy, Mn⁴⁺, and Mn²⁺. Al³⁺ is the predominant species across all samples. The fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest Al³⁺ fraction at 90.17 at.%, while the as-rolled S0 shows substantially higher Fe and Mn contents alongside reduced Al. As grain coarsening progresses from S950 to S1150, the Al³⁺ content progressively declines and Fe/Mn contents rise; the caption states that Al in solution-treated samples nonetheless remains higher than in S0. Fe is distributed as Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺_ox, and Fe³⁺_hy, whereas Mn presents as Mn²⁺ and Mn⁴⁺. These trends confirm that grain refinement drives preferential Al dissolution and dense Al-rich oxide film formation, enhancing corrosion resistance, whereas coarse-grained structures yield thinner, less Al-enriched films.

Fig. 15 presents Auger electron spectroscopy (AES) depth profiles of oxide films on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs). Panel (a) displays the atomic concentration of O, Fe, Al, and Mn versus sputtering time (0–12 min) for as-hot-rolled S0 and solution-treated samples S950, S1050, and S1150. Panel (b) reorganizes the same data by element, enabling direct comparison across all four samples. All profiles exhibit oxygen enrichment at the surface that decays toward the substrate composition with increasing sputter depth. The oxide film thicknesses, determined at half-maximum oxygen concentration, are visibly annotated as 16.2 ± 0.8 nm for S0, 25.2 ± 1.2 nm for S950, 21.6 ± 1 nm for S1050, and 18 ± 0.9 nm for S1150. The S950 film is the thickest and uniformly Al-rich with low Fe and Mn throughout the film, whereas S0 shows comparable Fe and Al concentrations, and S1050/S1150 exhibit increasing Fe and decreasing Al with depth. The figure supports the core conclusion that fine-grained S950 develops a uniform, dense, Al-rich oxide film, and caption states that both oxide film thickness and Al content decrease with increasing grain size.

This figure presents the thermodynamic E–pH (Pourbaix) diagrams for the Fe–Mn–Al–H₂O system at 25 °C calculated via HSC Chemistry 9 software, showing the stable regions of Al, Fe, and Mn species as functions of electrode potential (E vs. SHE, V) and pH under an assumed metal ion activity of 1×10⁻¹⁰ M and 1 atm gas pressure. The panel consists of three sub-diagrams: (a) Al, (b) Fe, and (c) Mn. Pink circles mark the experimental conditions of this study, located at approximately pH 6.5 and E ≈ −0.3 V. Under these conditions, Al falls within the Al₂O₃(H₂O) stability field, indicating a strong thermodynamic driving force for spontaneous formation of a dense, protective Al-rich oxide film. For Fe, the point lies in the Fe²⁺ dominant region near the FeO boundary, consistent with limited further oxidation due to low oxygen levels. For Mn, the point resides in the Mn²⁺ zone adjacent to the MnO₂ stability region, suggesting minimal thermodynamic tendency for oxidation to higher-valence Mn oxides. These diagrams collectively support that Al preferentially forms a stable protective oxide, while Fe and Mn remain primarily as dissolved cations or minor oxides during corrosion of the low-density steels.

Figure 17 presents the initial-stage corrosion dissolution behavior and surface potential distribution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with contrasting grain sizes. Panels (a) and (a1) show SEM micrographs of fine-grained sample S950 (solution-treated at 950 °C, ~8.7 μm average grain size) at scales of 5 μm and 1 μm, respectively, revealing both grain boundary dissolution and intragranular dissolution. Panels (b) and (b1) display corresponding SEM images of coarse-grained sample S1150 (solution-treated at 1150 °C, ~32.0 μm average grain size) at scales of 30 μm and 15 μm, similarly identifying preferential attack at grain boundaries. Panels (c) and (d) are SKPFM surface potential maps across an annealing twin boundary (ATB, Line A, 3 μm scale) and a high-angle grain boundary (HAGB, Line B, 2 μm scale). Panel (e) plots the potential profiles: Line A exhibits an elevated potential plateau (~10 to 15 mV) at the ATB, whereas Line B shows a sharp potential drop to approximately −20 mV at the HAGB, indicating that HAGBs are the most active anodic dissolution sites while ATBs possess a higher potential than the matrix. Collectively, these results demonstrate that fine-grained S950 possesses abundant grain boundaries that serve as initial dissolution channels, yet ultimately facilitate formation of a dense Al-rich oxide film, whereas the sparse boundaries in coarse-grained S1150 lead to slower, more discontinuous oxide film development.

This figure plots the polarization resistance (R_p) values derived from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) fittings of surface oxide films on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) versus immersion time up to 168 h. The graph compares four conditions: S0 (as-hot-rolled, red stars), S950 (fine-grained after 950 °C solution treatment, green circles), S1050 (intermediate grain size after 1050 °C, blue triangles), and S1150 (coarse-grained after 1150 °C, magenta squares). The S950 data display the highest R_p, rising sharply in the first 24 h and stabilizing near 1.2×10^5 Ω·cm²; citation text reports a value of 114.9 kΩ·cm² at 168 h. S1050 maintains intermediate values around 8.0×10^4 Ω·cm², whereas S1150 and S0 show substantially lower resistances, with S1150 reaching only 50.6 kΩ·cm² at 168 h. The markedly higher and faster-growing R_p for S950 indicates that fine grain size accelerates the formation of a dense, stable Al-rich oxide film, providing superior protection against corrosive media compared to coarse-grained microstructures.

Figure 19 presents cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comparing solution-treated samples S950 (950 °C), S1050 (1050 °C), and S1150 (1150 °C). Panel (a) shows the first cycle, where a distinct Al oxidation peak appears near −0.81 V_SCE; the fine-grained S950 exhibits the highest peak current density, whereas S1050 and S1150 display noticeably weaker peaks, as highlighted in the inset. No characteristic Fe oxidation peak is detected in the first cycle. Panel (b) presents the second cycle, revealing a persistent Al peak alongside a newly emerged Fe/Mn oxidation peak near −0.56 V_SCE. Notably, S950 shows the lowest Fe/Mn peak intensity, while S1050 and S1150 exhibit substantially enhanced peaks, indicating less protective surface films. These results demonstrate that the fine-grained S950 sample possesses superior Al oxidation activity and higher charge density during initial film formation, which rapidly generates a dense Al-rich oxide layer that effectively suppresses subsequent Fe and Mn oxidation, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance.

This figure presents schematic diagrams illustrating the oxide film formation and corrosion mechanisms in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes. Panels (a) and (a1)–(a3) depict the small grain size sample: (a) shows a fine-grained austenitic microstructure with abundant high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, blue lines), annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, red lines), and uniformly distributed metal elements (purple spheres); (a1) illustrates the surface after preferential dissolution at grain boundaries, forming a continuous pink oxide film embedding aluminum oxide (blue ellipses); the cross-sectional views (a2) to (a3) show the evolution from bare metal exposed to chloride-containing solution to a dense, continuous Al-rich oxide film. Panels (b) and (b1)–(b3) depict the large grain size sample: (b) shows a coarse-grained microstructure with sparse HAGBs and ATBs; (b1) reveals a discontinuous oxide film containing aluminum oxide, iron oxide (red ellipses), and manganese oxide (purple ellipses), accompanied by cracks; the cross-sectional sequence (b2) to (b3) demonstrates that the coarse-grained steel develops a thinner, defective oxide film with cracks and corrosion pits under chloride attack. The figure supports the conclusion that fine-grained structures facilitate rapid, continuous Al-rich oxide film formation via preferential intergranular dissolution, whereas coarse-grained structures suffer from discontinuous film growth and localized corrosion due to lower grain boundary density.
